title: Cameron vs Jonas: a points master against a veteran southpaw
summary: This super-welterweight meeting is a clash of methods. Chantelle Cameron's relentless scorecard boxing against the awkward, heavier-handed southpaw work of Natasha Jonas.
body:
This is a fight about how you win, not only whether you do. Chantelle Cameron has built a 21-1 record largely on the cards. Thirteen of her wins have gone to a decision and only eight have ended early, a knockout rate of 38 per cent. She wins rounds, banks them, and rarely needs the referee. Natasha Jonas offers something different in almost every respect.

Cameron: volume and the scorecards

At 35, Cameron arrives on a four-fight winning run, every one of them a decision. That is a fighter in rhythm, doing exactly what she does best. The low stoppage rate is part of her identity rather than a flaw in it. She is content to make a fight long and pile up the rounds, trusting her output to be there at the final bell.

Jonas: the southpaw veteran

Jonas, at 42, is the older fighter by seven years and the only southpaw in the ring. Her 16-3-1 record carries a higher finishing rate, nine knockouts at 56 per cent, and the stance alone changes the geometry. Orthodox against southpaw is the first puzzle both corners have to solve. Her recent five is more varied than Cameron's clean run, with a stoppage win in there but also a defeat.

What decides it

Can Jonas's southpaw angles and heavier hands disrupt a fighter who is happy to make it long? Long is precisely where Cameron's points pedigree lives. The shared height of 5ft 8in means neither can simply box over the top of the other. This one is settled in the exchanges and the accumulation, not the silhouette.
